class.name: SpatialAudioPlayer3D
class.brief_description: An advanced 3D audio player with automatic occlusion, reverb, air absorption, and water muffling.
class.description: [SpatialAudioPlayer3D] is a drop-in replacement for [AudioStreamPlayer3D] that adds physically-inspired spatial audio effects. It integrates with Blossom Engine's physics systems automatically. [b]Occlusion:[/b] Raycasts from the emitter to the listener detect walls in between. Each wall reduces volume and applies a lowpass filter. The amount of sound blocking is determined by the [PhysicsMaterial]'s [member PhysicsMaterial.penetration_resistance] — high resistance blocks more sound. [b]Reverb:[/b] Fibonacci-sphere distributed raycasts estimate the size of the surrounding room. Enclosed spaces produce more reverb, open areas produce less. The [PhysicsMaterial]'s [member PhysicsMaterial.reflection_factor] on surfaces controls how reflective they are acoustically. [b]Air absorption:[/b] High frequencies are naturally absorbed over distance. Sounds far away become duller, simulating real atmospheric absorption. [b]Water muffling:[/b] When the emitter or listener is inside a water volume (an [Area3D] with buoyancy properties), sound is muffled with a lowpass filter and volume reduction, simulating underwater audio. Each [SpatialAudioPlayer3D] creates its own dedicated audio bus with a lowpass filter and reverb effect. The bus is automatically cleaned up when the node exits the tree. [codeblock] # Use it like a normal AudioStreamPlayer3D: var player = SpatialAudioPlayer3D.new() player.stream = load("res://sounds/gunshot.wav") add_child(player) player.play() # Occlusion, reverb, and water muffling happen automatically. [/codeblock]

method.name: get_current_room_size
method.description: Returns the estimated room size as a normalized value between 0.0 (tiny enclosed space) and 1.0 (open area). Derived from the average distance of reverb raycasts.

method.name: get_current_wall_count
method.description: Returns the number of walls currently detected between the emitter and the listener. Updated every few physics frames based on [member occlusion_max_rays].

method.name: is_emitter_submerged
method.description: Returns [code]true[/code] if the emitter (this node) is currently inside a water volume.

method.name: is_listener_submerged
method.description: Returns [code]true[/code] if the listener (the active [Camera3D]) is currently inside a water volume.

members.member: If [code]true[/code], the player raycasts toward the listener each update to detect walls. Walls apply a lowpass filter and volume reduction based on the hit surface's [PhysicsMaterial].
Maximum number of walls the occlusion system can detect between emitter and listener. Each wall is a separate raycast step. Higher values catch more layered walls but cost more CPU.
The lowpass filter cutoff frequency (in Hz) when sound is fully occluded. Lower values produce more aggressive muffling. The filter smoothly interpolates between 20500 Hz (fully open) and this value.
Volume reduction in dB applied per occluding wall. A wall with low [member PhysicsMaterial.penetration_resistance] reduces volume less.
The physics collision mask used for occlusion and reverb raycasts. Only objects on matching layers are considered as occluders.
If [code]true[/code], the player estimates room size using Fibonacci-sphere raycasts and applies reverb automatically. Room size, wetness, and damping are derived from surrounding geometry.
Number of evenly-distributed raycasts used to estimate room size. More rays improve accuracy at higher CPU cost.
Maximum distance for reverb raycasts. Rays that don't hit anything within this distance are treated as open space.
Maximum reverb wetness. Acts as a ceiling on the wet signal regardless of how enclosed the space is.
If [code]true[/code], high frequencies are progressively filtered out as distance increases, simulating atmospheric absorption.
If [code]true[/code], sound is muffled when the emitter or listener is inside a water volume ([Area3D] with buoyancy properties). Applies both a lowpass filter and volume reduction.
Lowpass filter cutoff frequency (in Hz) applied when underwater. Lower values produce heavier muffling.

constants.constant: Volume decreases at a constant rate with distance.
Greater volume changes at close distances, lesser at far distances.
Inverse distance falloff. Only audible very close.
Middle-ground between logarithmic and inverse. Closest to real-world sound propagation.
